author: Laszlo
msg: For those who are curious, it's the minimum split for UNOs on the built-in marketplace on the app. For example, if you vote 50/50, none of the licenses can be below 50 for UNOs on the marketplace. However, you can still market your license for a better split to your ULOs and decide the split. This feature prevents a race to the bottom and gives UNOs room to market themselves. I voted 60/40 (if you already voted, it's still possible to retract your vote and revote)

Unity Vote Stereotypes

40/60 Voter: "It's just a side hustle, baby! I'm already financially free, so let's onboard everyone, the marketplace will handle the magic while I sip pina coladas. Zero stress, max growth!"

60/40 Voter: "I get it, I'm the marketer now! Over-invested? Guilty as charged, but I'll recruit ALL the ULOs to this life-changing project. Need that ROI to fuel my dreams... let's build this empire! 🚀"

author: Quoia75
msg:
People are already taking up licenses with 50/50 splits and even higher in favor of the UNO. Some in this group are just reluctant to accept that we are partially responsible for marketing these licenses.

Just a gentle reminder (again) all UNOs will be free to set whatever split they want on their private leases. Price controls in marketplaces is not something new or exotic. It's the bread and butter of wholes/retail. Without it markets collapse and influential wholesalers (us) aren't motivated to put forth their best effort to move product.

author: Joshua F
msg:
Hi all, while onboarding ULOs (especially non-technical users) I’ve seen the marketplace confuse people and sometimes stop them completing setup. I've submitted a feature request to simplify the default flow:

TL;DR: if splits have a minimum as per the latest poll and licences are effectively surfaced by an algorithm anyway, the marketplace layer may be redundant and adds friction. A simple “claim” system could improve onboarding for everyone.

Feature proposal:

This proposal replaces the default marketplace experience with a simple in-app licence claim flow designed for new users. On first use, a user either enters an optional referral code or skips it; if they skip, the app randomly assigns a licence from the available pool at a standardised 60% UNO / 40% ULO split. If they enter a valid referral code (one unique custom code per UNO), they claim a licence and receive a +10% rewards uplift, making the effective split 50% UNO / 50% ULO. Licences are effectively indefinite provided the user maintains at least 80% uptime, and the UNO can only revoke if uptime drops below that threshold. Separately, UNOs can still publish custom UNO/ULO splits that users claim manually using the longer codes we already use today, keeping advanced flexibility without forcing it into the default user journey.

It’s needed because the marketplace adds avoidable friction and decision fatigue at the exact point where non-technical users are most likely to churn. Standardising the default split eliminates race-to-the-bottom dynamics and prevents incentives from drifting towards short-term undercutting. A one-tap claim flow makes onboarding materially easier for people outside the existing crypto-native circles, while the referral uplift creates a clear, measurable incentive for UNOs to market and actively bring in new participants. In short, it improves conversion and retention by simplifying the path to first rewards, while preserving customisation for power users via the manual route.

author: Quoia75
msg:
*Caveat - __this request included an additional option to auto upgrade or downgrade ULOs through earning levels before they would have a license auto-revoked. There was also a more plain vanilla version that just requested having an auto revoke feature.__

— Submitted November 6, 2025 —

**Enable automatic tier upgrades and license revoking based on up-time requirements**
After an operator sets up several earning tiers (i.e. 30%, 50%, 70%, etc.) they will have the option to also tie these payment tiers to a required percentage of up-time for the licenses to be retained. 

Examples below:

__Tier 2
Earning potential: 50%
Up-time Requirement: at least 50% up-time for 60 days__

If the licensee falls below this requirement for a consecutive number of days (let’s say 5, this should be customizable) then the license will be auto-downgraded to the next lowest tier. If they continue to fall below the required up-time threshold for each tier they will eventually have their license revoked and they will have to request a new one (lowest tier).

Conversely, if they are meeting the up-time requirements for their current tier, they should be auto-upgraded to the next highest earning tier (example 80% for 60 days). As long as they are able to maintain a threshold of good uptime for the highest tier, they will be rewarded by being able to keep the license for perpetuity. 

In this scenario, all licenses are made available at the lowest tier first, and excellent uptime is rewarded by upgrades to a higher earning tier, and being able to retain the license indefinitely.

No unfortunately you do not get it. Marketing wise we must start high and move lower. The other way around never works, except when we are a monopoly or the demand greatly outperforms supply. Fortunately this will be the end game for Unity, but until we get there, marketing wise, we need to start high and have the option to promote lower, "special" deals.

Furthermore, since in the early stages, UNOs will drive adoptions with their efforts, they  need to be incentivized. If we were in a state were rewards were high enough so as this thing to market itself, I would agree with you. But we are not there, and manual (UNO) actions need support.

Do not forget, the team has stated that they expect from us, UNO, to promote this thing. At least, we should allow ourselves to do what is expected from us as effectively as possible. Not voting for the 60/40 is like shooting your own foot, marketing wise. This will only result in dragging the project adoption down until it can eventually market itself.

Think about it.

author: Byron
msg:
regarding the "external" marketplaces .. that I have read about the last few hours. This is totally ridiculous. What part of MASS ADOPTION you do not understand? Let me say it one more time.

In order to achieve mass adoption the ULO needs to be onboarded in just 2 steps:

1) Enter their email to register
2) Validate their email, and automatically get assigned a license from the marketplace and start PoW

All other options have to be optional (enter affiliate code, enter license code, browse the marketplace) and preferably out of the "mainstream" onboarding process.

For mass adoption, only one marketplace matters. The default Unity marketplace. Anything else will be for the initiated, which will be the vast minority of ULOs.

Mass adoption means —> Download Unity app from Apple/Play store, register and earn
Mass adoption does not mean —> Download Unity app from Apple/Play store, go to site A, browse for a license with a "benefiting" profile (learn about UNO/ULO splits, uptimes, lease durations), get the license code, register to the Unity app, redeem license code, start eanring

author: Mike
msg:
I don't really follow all the 60/40 supporters, saying it'll allow UNO's to market for a lower split...
That way UNO's who do extra work get less rewards than UNO's who don't market themselves.

Meanwhile without a minimum (or a lower one), the licenses that get listed cheaply, will quickly get picked up, automatically raising the floor.
This lets UNO's, who want to do extra marketing, to do so for a higher split. This means the UNO's who do more, will get higher rewards.
You could even argue that it makes their job of marketing easier, because there's already an existing network of ULO's that are getting great rewards.
